entry.title: SAP TechEd 2024 - Summary
entry.content:
SAP TechEd 2024 delivered an inspiring keynote with a strong focus on how technology drives real-world business innovations. This session brought to light the latest advances in AI, cloud solutions, and SAP's vision for the future of enterprise software.

The keynote opened with a thought-provoking introduction about the human brain, its complexities, and how innovation emerges from millions of connections forming new ideas. This metaphor seamlessly tied into SAP's philosophy of turning ideas into tangible solutions—where concepts become tools for solving real business problems.

Innovation at the Heart of SAP’s Strategy

One of the central themes was the growing importance of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in SAP’s offerings. AI is now deeply integrated into SAP’s cloud-driven portfolio, designed to simplify customers' lives and meet evolving business needs. Senior AI Scientists at SAP are working on harnessing AI to make business software smarter and more intuitive. The keynote also emphasized SAP's efforts to ensure that its software is configurable, extensible, and unified, providing a seamless suite-like experience across its platforms.

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)

A significant highlight was SAP's Business Technology Platform (BTP), which has seen rapid growth in customer adoption. BTP is the cornerstone for SAP’s cloud and AI-driven future, empowering businesses with application development, automation, data analytics, and AI capabilities all within one unified platform. The keynote highlighted that over 27,000 live customers and 2,800 partners are leveraging SAP BTP to drive innovation.

The announcement of new BTP regions running on infrastructure from partners like AWS, Microsoft, and Google, along with SAP-managed data centers, shows SAP’s commitment to providing broader regional coverage and reliable service. This expansion aims to help businesses operate globally while maintaining efficiency, security, and compliance.

Enhancements in Application Development and Automation

SAP unveiled its Clean Core Strategy, which allows businesses to build custom extensions in a way that keeps the core ERP system maintainable and upgradeable. With SAP’s low-code environment, developers can create AI-powered applications with ease, while ABAP Cloud now supports both on-stack and side-by-side development, giving businesses more flexibility in their customizations.

One exciting feature introduced was the SAP Build Extensibility Wizard, a tool designed to streamline the process of creating custom fields and extensions, allowing users to enhance SAP applications with minimal coding.

SAP Build and GenAI

In keeping with the AI theme, SAP showcased the GenAI capabilities within SAP Build. With Joule, an AI assistant, developers can create complex data models, build extensions, and generate applications through natural language prompts. This AI-driven approach reduces manual coding and helps users integrate advanced functionalities like time series forecasting and predictive analytics into their business processes.

Data and Analytics Innovations

The keynote further highlighted innovations in data and analytics, specifically focusing on SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Analytics Cloud, and SAP Datasphere. The HANA Cloud Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph Engine were introduced, both of which enable businesses to harness structured and unstructured data for more context-aware AI-driven decisions. These engines promise faster and more accurate data processing, particularly in scenarios involving large language models and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).

Additionally, the keynote showcased how SAP Datasphere is being adopted by large enterprises like Hershey’s and Bosch for data cataloging, integration, and self-service analytics, empowering businesses to access trusted data with ease.

Integration Suite and AI-Driven Optimization

The SAP Integration Suite now offers a modern, AI-powered solution for real-time end-to-end business process integration. With thousands of pre-built integrations and connectors, businesses can seamlessly integrate SAP and third-party applications. SAP is also leveraging AI-generated recommendations to optimize custom scripts, further enhancing developer productivity and reducing memory consumption.

Conclusion

SAP TechEd 2024 demonstrated SAP’s unwavering commitment to making innovation accessible, impactful, and transformative. From the integration of advanced AI capabilities to the expansion of BTP, SAP is setting a new standard in enterprise software. Whether you are looking to enhance your application development, automate processes, or harness the power of data, SAP’s latest innovations offer powerful tools to bring your ideas to life.

As SAP continues to evolve, the future of enterprise technology is more connected, intelligent, and adaptable than ever before.

Here is the Keynote from SAP:

Comment below your thoughts on TechEd.

entry.title: #04 - Launch Your Project: The Broader Perspective Digital Transformation
entry.content:
Transforming your business with cloud technology calls for a paradigm shift in the way you work, think, plan, and execute. This is an exciting and rewarding journey, which requires the right strategy, effort, and investments to get all your people on the same page. Collaboration across different teams and experts is essential to ensure a successful project and to achieve the outcomes you set out for the organization.

A move to the cloud is more than just a software implementation, automating how the business works. It involves changing and adapting core – often long-standing – processes and workflows to meet changing business goals, remain competitive, and meet customer demands. It is about an innovative and business focused approach to redefine how work is done so that it helps the organization succeed. Technology is probably THE most important enabler of the transformation NOT the transformation per se.

The potential of Business Transformation, which implementing any cloud technology brings, will only be determined by establishing a future to present strategy and the efforts being made to drive it.

Enabling Business Transformation

According to the recommendations of many global organizations that already call themselves “successful” on their cloud enabled transformation plus the studies from other reputable sources we say that to enable meaningful Business Transformation results organizations are required to activate the right strategies and programs to accompany their cloud technology implementation and sustainment efforts.

Amazing business results are possible but do not come like magic just because an organization acquires SuccessFactors. “Magic” is the a result of having the right technologies, a future to present mindset and defining the right transformation programs and resources to your organization’s needs.

The below Business Transformation Framework highlights the various programs to help with a successful Business Transformation. 

Copyright - Customer Transformation Advisory, SAP SuccessFactors

The Business Transformation Framework encompasses 11 important elements that need to play altogether to achieve expected results:

1. Business Transformation Foundation:

The Foundational elements of the Business Transformation Framework, clarify the purpose of the transformation focusing on the Strategic elements which need to be well defined and communicated. This ensures the direction is well set and all actions should focus on achieving desired outcomes and business goals, which tie up with your overall business strategy and which provide the guiding principles for the way technology is configured and integrated:

1.1. Strategy and Digital Plan:

Establish the future direction and business value focusing on key priorities, goals, and outcomes for the overall business but specially for the business area that is adopting the new technology.  A successful Digital Strategy Plan considers the strategy of the specific business area and then defines where, when and how the use of technology would be essential to drive tangible results.

1.2. Organization and Operating Model:

Define the right Organizational Structure with value-add programs, processes, roles and skills. Does the organization and/or the business area need to revamp itself to deliver value to the internal and external stakeholders? What are the specific skill sets required?  It is best to adopt a “future to present mindset” when designing a model to execute programs and processes for the business with the right people and structure, enabled through a cloud-based solution.

1.3. Stakeholder Experience:

Design the processes to capture the future expectations of your stakeholders what do we want them to feel and sense on their journey?. Focus on the “moments that matter”. Involve your stakeholders in the implementation process earlier on, to help ensure user satisfaction and adoption, and avoid resistance and conflict. To ensure you are continuously reinforcing change and adoption, continue to think from the point of view of your personas and what matters to them.  

2. Operational Excellence:

This refers to the Tactical Programs that need to be in place on a continuous fashion to drive the transformation journey. These define the actions required to achieve your business goals and targets. This set of initiatives should be managed by a core dedicated team and supported by the business defined stakeholders. Let us look at these in detail.

2.1. Executive Sponsorship:

Having an executive sponsor lends credibility to transformation projects and highlights their strategic importance within the organization. It also ensures that resources as needed will be in place and obstacles will be removed or at least reduced at some point. Securing the right sponsor ensures strong leadership support, aids in communicating initiatives, securing necessary resources and budgets, and ensures full organizational buy-in.

2.2. Cloud Culture:

Organizations that embrace a cloud culture focus not only on their technological infrastructure but also on transforming their work processes. They invest in cultivating a new mindset and approach to technology where people is open and prepared to work with technology. To fully leverage the benefits of the cloud, fostering the right attitudes and behaviors among stakeholders is essential. This forms the foundation for successfully achieving business transformation goals.

2.3. Change Management:

The goal of change management is to implement a structured, continuous program that helps people in the organization transition from its current state to a desired future state. This program should focus on people, understanding the target personas, their behaviors, and what motivates them. It should remain relevant even after go-live. A key element is to address the three Vs—Vision, Value, and Vested Interest—of everyone involved and affected by the change through different initiatives as communication, training and sensitization.

According to an SAP Study, employees are more likely to adopt solutions if they help them achieve their goals. The image below illustrates the 3Ws for building trust in technology and encouraging better adoption among your workforce:

2.4. Governance Model:

Designing a program that fosters effective decision-making, ensures organizational alignment, and mitigates risks, should be a priority when designing any transformation project's Governance Model. A strong governance framework is essential for managing technology responsibly and efficiently. The model should begin with a clear purpose statement, include guiding principles, and outline specific goals and outcomes, with defined accountability and ownership for each decision.

Additionally, here’s a sample template of a Model Charter for an Executive Committee within an organization.

2.5. Sustainment:

Establish a program to manage and sustain both processes and technology solutions. Technology is never implemented solely to address current challenges; it's always an investment with the future in mind. Sustainment includes planning for ongoing support, adoption strategies, release management, and innovation management. During and after implementation, it's crucial to formally define the roles, responsibilities, and processes required to keep your solution updated, relevant, and continuously delivering business value. A simple RACI matrix can be a good starting point to monitor daily tasks, defining who is Accountable (Owner), Responsible (Doer), Consulted (as needed), and Informed (as required). It is very important to evolve the roles, responsibilities and processes identified during the implementation phase once Live with your solution in order to keep it updated, relevant and producing continued value and desired outcomes.

2.6. Value Management:

Establishing an approach to identify, track, and measure both quantitative and qualitative transformation outcomes is essential. The value management framework focuses on defining the metrics that align with our goals and objectives. It's important to identify and agree on the business KPIs that will drive the most significant outcomes, track them consistently, and measure progress against expectations

2.7. Optimization and Innovation:

This dimension refers to building an approach to advance people, processes, and technology transformation. With Cloud products, innovation is rapid and so should be their adoption. To enable agility under this dimension of the Business Transformation Framework, focus on activities such as evaluating optimization vs innovation within your organization. Innovation introduces new ideas and emerging technology into an existing product, process or service whilst optimization adds incremental improvements to an existing product, process or service. Another focus area should be the ideation of a new strategy which ties in with that of the overall business’ in the future, and with that, make sure to reimagine the programs that are going to be needed to support this new strategy. Conduct a business and functional alignment refresh by ensuring to update common goals, which will serve as the north star for the future direction of current projects and/or sustainment efforts and communicate these transparently. Finally, establish an innovation roadmap by creating a visual representation that aligns the organization’s strategy with innovation actions and brings clarity to the direction, processes, priorities, and timeline required to achieve specific goals. 

2.8. Technology Framework:

Align organizational principles for digital transformation and define tactics to boost adoption and usability for a better experience. The successful implementation of the project would have a lot of technological considerations, like Data Management, aligning with other transformation initiatives within the organization, Technology Roadmap, Network Devices and Mobile functionality. The team members who play a role in implementing the solution, need to make educated and informed decisions. This would mean, the teams to develop strong competencies according to their role, for them to be aware of the Best/ Leading Practices and be able to learning from the proven success of previous implementations and avoiding pitfalls.

3. Technology as the “Enabler”:

Technology will then be the FOUNDATION to support your business strategy Technology will act as both a driver of innovation and a tool for execution. Companies can leverage market leading technology to create digital ecosystems that bring together customers, suppliers, and partners, employee, improving collaboration and creating new value for everyone in the value chain. By aligning technological investments and implementations with business goals, companies can drive innovation, optimize processes, improve customer engagement, and ultimately, create sustainable competitive advantages.

Check out some helpful resources to further aid you with your Business Transformation journey!

  • Learn how organizational change management can contribute to the success of your SAP cloud project through the learning journey Discover Organizational Change Management for SAP Cloud Project and the Change Management Roadmap - Roadmap Viewer (sap.com)
  • Are you implementing any of the SAP SuccessFactors HCM solutions? Register for our Road to Adoption Webcast series to better understand how organizations are making changes to drive adoption and successful business outcomes.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Check out other blogs from the series: Customer Onboarding at SAP.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

About the authors:

  • Aarthi Iyer is an SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding Advisor and Events Lead, and her current role is to guide SuccessFactors customers through a successful implementation journey. With experience across various HCM roles, she has led multiple end-to-end implementation projects, architecting effective solutions for customers. Her expertise lies in guiding clients through successful HR transformation projects and helping them unlock long term value from their investments.
  • Vanessa Alvarado, 8+ years at SAP working as Global Customer Transformation Advisor and before that as CEE based in Mexico. Passionate for supporting customers all around the world to better define their transformation strategy enabled by HXM technology.
  • Nikki Pardo is a Senior Customer Onboarding Advisor within the SAP HCM Customer Onboarding Team, providing guidance, enablement, and services focused on developing HCM Customers’ internal experts to deliver successful implementations and achieve their key business goals. With more than 16 years of experience as a HR business professional and another 6 within the SuccessFactors field, Nikki’s interest in people, efficiencies, new technologies and organisational development allow her to expand on her passion for helping companies and individuals overcome the psychological and operational obstacles that stand in the way of reaching their full potential.

Having questions, comments or feedbacks? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @aarthiiyer , @VaneA_Transformation or @narkixepardo.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

entry.title: Configuring OAuth 2.0 between SAP C4C and SAP BTP
entry.content:
You need to create HTTP destinations in the SAP BTP cockpit. These destinations provide data communication via HTTP protocol.

You can use the SAML Bearer assertion flow for consuming OAuth-protected resources. Users are authenticated by using SAML against the configured trusted identity providers. The SAML assertion is then used to request an access token from an OAuth authorization server. This access token must be added as an Authorization header with value Bearer <access token> in all HTTP requests to the OAuth-protected resources.

WORKFLOW

  1. Configure OAuth Identity Provider in SAP Cloud for Customer.
  2. Configure the OAuth Client for OData Access to SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs.
  3. Create and Configure the HTTP Destination.

1. Configure OAuth Identity Provider in SAP Cloud for Customer:

  1. Log on to the SAP BTP cockpit, and configure the settings as below,
    1. Navigate to your extension subaccount in the Cloud Foundry environment.
    2. Choose Connectivity ->Destination Trust.
    3. Choose Export to get the active trust certificate for this subaccount and save it on your local file system.                                                              
    4. Open the certificate in a text editor, copy the content between -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE-----, and save it in a file with the following format <subaccount>_signing.cer, where <subaccount> is the Subaccount Name from the Subaccount Information of your subaccount.
  2. Log on to your SAP Cloud for Customer system as an administrator. Go to ADMINISTRATOR ->Common Tasks.
  3. Choose Configure OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider ->New OAuth2.0 Provider, and configure the settings as below,                                                                                                                                            
    1. In the Issuing Entity Name field, enter cfapps.<region_host>/<subaccountID>. You go to the SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to the subaccount, go to Overview and,
      • For the <region_host>, copy the API endpoint from the Cloud Foundry section, and remove the https://api.cf..
      • For the <subaccountID>, copy the ID from the Subaccount Details section.
      • For example: cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/12345678-1234-1234-1234-1234578.
    2. From the Primary Signing Certificate field, browse the <subaccount>_signing.cer file that you saved.
    3. Select the E-Mail Address checkbox.
    4. Choose Submit.

Here is an example for OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider:                                 

2. Configure the OAuth Client for OData Access to SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs:

  1. Log on to your SAP Cloud for Customer system as an administrator.
  2. Go to ADMINISTRATOR ->OAUTH2.0 CLIENT REGISTRATION.
  3. Choose New,                                                        
  4. Specify a client secret, description, and token lifetime (in seconds). For example, 3600 seconds.
  5. In the Issuer Name field, use the dropdown list to specify the identity provider that you created.
  6. Copy the entry in the Client ID field. You will need it later when creating the HTTP destination with OAuth authentication required for the connectivity to the SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs.                                     
  7. In the Scope list, select the UIWC:CC_HOME scope.
  8. Choose Save and Close.                                            

3. Create and Configure the HTTP Destination:

  1. Log on to your SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your subaccount.
  2. Choose Connectivity ->Destinations in the navigation panel.
  3. Create an HTTP destination.
  4. To enable principal propagation, create an OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion HTTP destination,                                     
    1. Configure the basic settings:                                                                     
      ParameterValue
      NameEnter a meaningful name.
      TypeHTTP
      Description(Optional) Enter a meaningful description.
      URLhttps://<my_SAP_Cloud_for_Customer_system_name>.crm.ondemand.com/sap/c4c/odata/v1/c4codataapi
      Proxy TypeInternet
      AuthenticationOAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion
      AudienceGo to the SAP Cloud for Customer administration view, then navigate to Configure Single Sign-On under General Settings and copy the value from the Local Service Provider field.
      Client KeyClient ID
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Token Service URLhttps://<my_SAP_Cloud_for_Customer_system_name>.crm.ondemand.com/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token
      Token Service UserClient ID
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Token Service PasswordClient secret
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client Secret field when configuring the OAuth client.
    2. Configure the required additional property. To do so, in the Additional Properties panel, choose New Property, and enter the following property:                                                                                   
      ParameterValue
      scopeScope ID entries separated by space.
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Example: UIWC:CC_HOME
    3. Select the Use default JDK truststore checkbox.
    4. Save your entries.

Here is an example for a destination definition and additional parameters:

In this way, we can configure the OAuth 2.0 between SAP Cloud for Customer and SAP Business Technology Platform.

Thanks,
Arun

entry.title: LEVEREDGE 2024 Highlights: Discovering New Strategies for Digital Transformation
entry.content:
Organizations face a multitude of challenges today, from adapting to rapid technological advancements to navigating complex regulatory environments. LEVEREDGE 2024 was created to address these pressing issues and to provide a platform where industry leaders come together to explore the latest strategies, technologies, and solutions shaping the future of business.

Over three days, this premier event gathered 400+ guests and thought leaders from 10+ countries to share insights, experiences, and success stories. Attendees had the opportunity to engage in diverse sessions, including keynote presentations, panel discussions, and interactive workshops, covering vital topics such as Business AI, Digital Supply Chain, Intelligent Spend & Procurement, Data Migration, S/4HANA, Product Lifecycle Management, and much more.

Key Highlights of Day 1

The event kicked off with a Design Thinking Networking Breakfast, setting the tone for meaningful connections among participants. This engaging atmosphere fostered discussions that sparked new ideas and collaborative solutions.

The day continued with Design Thinking Workshops led by the LeverX AppHaus Team and Andreas Hauser, Senior Vice President of the SAP AppHaus Network, where participants explored AI-driven solutions.

Further collaborative discussions emphasized the potential of AI and fostered a spirit of collaboration, encouraging attendees to address the challenges businesses face on their digital transformation journeys.

Key Highlights of Day 2

Day 2 of LEVEREDGE 2024 was all about deepening our understanding of the latest advancements in digital transformation, artificial intelligence, and innovative business practices.

Spanning three dynamic stages — the Blue Stage, Green Stage, and the Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) — the agenda featured keynotes from industry leaders, expert panels, and interactive discussions, each offering valuable perspectives on the evolving role of technology in today’s business landscape. From AI-powered solutions to groundbreaking PLM innovations, the day was packed with insightful conversations, practical takeaways, and plenty of opportunities to network with peers and thought leaders.

 

🔵 Blue Stage highlights

Opening remarks: Motivational speeches from Dr. Victor Lozinski, Co-Founder and Chairman of the Board at LeverX, and Daniella Levine Cava, Mayor of Miami-Dade County.

  • Maximizing business efficiency: How leveraging SAP technology can unlock new levels of operational efficiency and enhance customer experiences.
  • AI in research & development: The role of AI in accelerating research and development processes, particularly in the pharmaceutical sector, driving faster innovation.
  • AI & digitalization for business sustainability: An exploration of how AI and digitalization are transforming business models, driving sustainability, and supporting growth in various industries.
  • Data modernization: The critical importance of modernizing data strategies to maintain a competitive edge in today’s rapidly evolving market.
  • Innovative service solutions: Multi-platform service applications that deliver enhanced operational capabilities and customer value were showcased.
  • Leadership & technology: How effective leadership can help organizations navigate technological advancements and leverage them for business growth.

🟢 Green Stage highlights

  • AI-powered Clean Core extensions: How SAP Business Technology Platform enhances key extensions with AI to drive innovation and operational efficiency.
  • Cybersecurity and AI: An exploration of the collaborative role of AI and Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) solutions in improving SAP cybersecurity strategies.
  • Generative AI in enterprise operations: The benefits of integrating generative AI solutions from cloud platforms like AWS to transform enterprise operations.
  • ERP transformation success: A case study on the successful implementation of S/4HANA to streamline ERP transformation in the manufacturing sector.
  • Warehousing innovations: Emerging trends and innovations in SAP warehousing solutions that optimize supply chain processes.
  • Enhanced employee experiences: The discussion on how mobile technology, AI, and user-centered design to improve employee engagement and experiences.
  • HR transformation with SAP SuccessFactors: The journey of implementing SAP SuccessFactors to drive operational excellence in human resource management.
  • Digital transformation in the food industry: A real-world example of digital transformation within the food and beverage industry, focusing on improving operational efficiency and customer experience.

🟡Yellow Stage highlights

  • SAP PLM vision & strategy: An exploration of the latest developments and future strategies for SAP Product Lifecycle Management (PLM), focusing on innovation and business growth.
  • Global PLM implementation success: A real-world case study demonstrating the successful implementation of a global PLM system in discrete industries, highlighting its transformative effects on product lifecycle management.
  • Panel on PLM trends and challenges: A discussion of key trends in digital twins, product design, and the critical role of integrating PLM into enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems to drive business efficiency.

Breakout sessions:

  • Specification management & formulation: A deep dive into process PLM, focusing on the complexities of managing product specifications and formulation processes.
  • Integrating digital twins in PLM and ERP: An exploration of how digital twins are becoming an essential component of product design and how they integrate with SAP's PLM and ERP solutions.

 

As Day 2 of LEVEREDGE 2024 came to a close, a clear theme emerged: the critical role that AI, digital transformation, and innovative leadership play in shaping the future of business. As industries continue to innovate, the integration of AI, data-driven solutions, and leadership will remain key drivers of sustainable growth and transformation.

Key Highlights of Day 3

Day 3 of LEVEREDGE 2024 was all about embracing the transformative power of AI, digital transformation, and leadership innovation. As the final day of the event unfolded, two primary stages — the Blue Stage and the Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) — hosted a range of insightful sessions. 

🔵 Blue Stage highlights

  • AI for business efficiency: Introduction to the practical integration of AI into business operations, focusing on enhancing efficiency and scalability.
  • AI in logistics: Insights on how AI is transforming logistics, improving delivery precision, and optimizing supply chain management.
  • Leadership and innovation: A session exploring the intersection of leadership and technology, offering lessons from leadership journeys and innovative business practices.
  • Digital transformation case studies: Real-world examples of how businesses are leveraging AI, mobile technology, and SAP solutions to drive digital transformation and operational excellence.
  • AWS and SAP BTP for innovation: Discussion on how AWS and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) are enabling organizations to innovate and streamline processes for better business outcomes.
  • Mobile work order management: A demonstration of the technology behind mobile work order management, showcasing its ability to simplify workflows and enhance operational efficiency.

AI-powered solutions in action: Use cases illustrating how AI-powered solutions are driving industry transformation, with examples of businesses adopting AI to enhance their operations.

  • Digital transformation in transportation: Insights into how companies are using SAP S/4HANA and Transportation Management to achieve operational efficiencies in the transportation sector.
  • Maximizing SAP investments: An exploration of the strategic benefits of engaging with user groups to fully unlock the potential of SAP investments.
  • Managing vendor and supply chain risks: Best practices for navigating risks in vendor and supply chain management, with a focus on procurement and mitigation strategies.

🟡 Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) highlights

  • Sustainable innovation: An exploration of how PLM drives innovation while ensuring sustainability and compliance.
  • AI in product development: How AI improves the efficiency of new product development processes.
  • PLM success stories: Real-world examples of PLM implementations, showcasing business transformation.

PLM security: Best practices for securing PLM applications and protecting data.

  • Product data governance: Managing product data and engineering changes effectively.
  • Industry-specific roadmaps: Future advancements in PLM for discrete and process industries.

As Day 3 concluded, a key takeaway was the undeniable impact of AI and digital transformation on industries across the board. From revolutionizing logistics and supply chains to driving sustainable innovation through PLM solutions, the sessions underscored how technology is reshaping modern business practices. The importance of leadership, collaboration, and continuous learning was evident, as attendees left with fresh perspectives and strategies to implement in their own organizations. LEVEREDGE 2024 concluded with a clear message: innovation, driven by AI and digital tools, is the future of business success.

The Bottom Line

LEVEREDGE 2024 showcased the future of business, emphasizing the pivotal role of AI, digital transformation, and leadership in driving innovation. Across three days of insightful sessions, industry leaders and experts shared their visions and practical strategies for navigating the evolving business landscape. From AI-powered logistics to sustainable PLM solutions, the event highlighted how technology is reshaping industries. As attendees leave equipped with fresh perspectives, the key takeaway is clear: embracing innovation and leveraging digital tools are essential for achieving lasting business success.

entry.title: Sustainability in Defense and Security: Why It’s Essential
entry.content:
Why Sustainability Matters

 

“We simply must do everything we can in our power to slow down global warming before it is too late. We can save our planet and also boost our economy at the same time.”

 

You may now be wondering what this has to do with Defense and Security organizations such as armed forces, firefighters, police, ambulance services, or aid organizations. The answer is simply more than most people think. For one thing, according to the leading consultancy McKinsey, D&S organizations account for approximately 50% of governments’ carbon emissions from fleets and facilities in countries like Canada and the United Kingdom, and up to about 80% in the United States of America. On the other hand, if you consider global CO2 emissions, the military sector accounts for 1-5%. Although this stake does not seem high at first glance, it certainly does when you set it in relation to other business sectors and see that the emissions are comparably high as in the aviation or shipping industry. This means that any government that aims to reduce its overall emissions needs to consider its defense forces in its climate action plan too. For another thing, current, state-of-the-art sustainability solutions do not really harm the organizational performance or the practical operational readiness of military organizations anymore. This is especially because key processes, such as the measurement of emissions, are usually run in the background through automated calculations and the power of highly innovative sensor technology and software. But more on that later. Before that, I want to inform you about some additional statistical data and facts about sustainability. The consulting company Boston Consulting Group (BCG) found that the global defense industry’s share in global CO2 emissions could rise significantly from 2% in 2021 to 25% in 2050 if the industry does not take active action to reduce its emissions. But what do these emissions look like in detail?

 

Figure 1: CO2 Emissions of the US Department of Defense (2023)

 

As the above pie chart of the CO2 emissions of the US Department of Defense in 2023 shows, most emissions result from vehicles and equipment or purchased electricity. In Germany, however, where the emissions of the Bundeswehr increased by 17.9% in the period of 2019 to 2021, we see a somewhat different picture since most of the increase results from increased fuel consumption of the air force as well as increased heating requirements of the Bundeswehr’s military infrastructure. Interestingly, as the well-known Nature magazine pointed out in an article, many military organizations around the world still do not measure and report their emissions in as much detail as other governmental organizations or private companies do. In contrast, 95% of the largest companies globally publish ESG (environmental, social, and governance) reports regularly.

 

What Actions are Required to meet the Climate Targets?

 

However, although the above statistics may not look really promising to the reader, we luckily see that the problem was recognized a long time ago by many Defense & Security organizations. Military organizations such as the US Department of Defense, the German Bundeswehr, or NATO have actively created climate action plans within the last few years:

 

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in this regard set itself the following targets in 2021:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 45% by 2030.
  • Achievement of net zero emissions (Net zero emissions occur when the amount of greenhouse gases emitted equals the amount removed from the atmosphere) by 2050.

 

The European Union, on the other hand, has set the defense sector within the Union similar climate action goals:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels.
  • Carbon neutrality by 2050.
  • Increased use of renewable energies and focus on improvement of energy efficiency within military operations.
  • Implementation of sustainable practices in defense operations.
  • Passing of a so-called “Climate-Security Nexus”, that addresses the link between climate change and security threats, thereby including climate considerations into defense planning and operations.

 

As can be seen in the text above, there are already a number of promising strategic plans, that include proven concepts and best practices for reducing greenhouse gas emissions of military organizations. However, specialized management consultancies such as BCG and McKinsey in this regard have drawn up a number of additional recommendations for making defense organizations more sustainable:

 

Recommendations from McKinsey

McKinsey suggests leveraging low-carbon technologies by using electric vehicles, sustainable aviation fuel, and renewable energy sources. Additionally, the consultancy recommends putting the focus mainly on non-mission-critical areas. This according to McKinsey allows armed forces organizations to achieve a 60% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions. Other suggestions from the company include to establish an end-to-end consideration of sustainability in value chains and supply chains of Defense and Security organizations.

 

Recommendations from the Boston Consulting Group:

The US-based consulting company Boston Consulting Company (BCG) principally makes similar suggestions. It’s main recommendation is to make significant investments into sustainable technologies through research and development. Furthermore, it highly recommends D&S organizations to enhance emissions reporting and accountability as well as to pursue net-zero emission goals.

 

Other Recommendations and Initiatives:

The consulting company KPMG suggests D&S organizations to align their climate goals and actions with international and national climate targets, whereas Nature advocates for mandatory emissions reporting and international agreements and methodologies to monitor and reduce military emissions. Additionally, D&S organizations such as the US DoD or the US Army have established their own guidelines for water efficiency and water security.

 

Sustainability Targets of Emergency Organizations:

These recommendations are not only true for military organizations, but are applicable to emergency organizations like Police, Ambulances or Firefighting organizations in a similar manner. Examples include organizations such as the London Fire Brigade, which has outlined a “Sustainable Development Strategy for 2023-2025” and consequently invested in measures like acquiring electric vehicles and making their fire stations more energy efficient. Other examples in this direction include the Berlin Police, that has implemented measures such as using hybrid and electric cars along with sustainable practices such as a focus on recycling and a reduction of paper use. In doing so the emergency organization aims to support the City of Berlin in reaching its climate reduction 2030 and climate neutrality 2045 goals.

 

Given the above-mentioned challenges and facts, I now want to draw the reader’s attention to the holistic SAP Sustainability Solutions, that allow D&S organizations to actively tackle and manage all common aspects of sustainability.  

 

The SAP Sustainability Solutions

 

 

Video 1: ERP to ESG: How to develop your first ESG Strategy

 

SAP is proud to be one of the leading software suppliers for Enterprise Resource Planning and other software solutions for various leading companies and organizations around the world. However, what is less well-known across customers and industries is that SAP also offers powerful and holistic cross-industry sustainability software solutions. The leading technology research companies, Gartner and IDC, have recognized SAP’s sustainability solutions for their comprehensive capabilities. Gartner has rated SAP as a preferred vendor for carbon accounting and management software solutions, while IDC has praised the “breadth and depth” of SAP’s offerings. The SAP solutions are thereby not only highly capable of measuring, analyzing, and managing carbon emissions, but are also trusted by leading companies and organizations worldwide, such as IBM, PWC, Freudenberg, msg global, and SAP itself. SAP’s sustainability solutions feature real-time data analytics, seamless integration with existing ERP systems, and AI-driven insights to help businesses make informed decisions. For example, IBM has leveraged SAP’s solutions to significantly reduce its carbon footprint, while PWC has used them to enhance its sustainability reporting. SAP’s commitment to sustainability has also been recognized with several industry awards, and the company continues to innovate with new features and developments on the horizon.

In general, the SAP Sustainability Cloud software suite is built on the following four main software solutions:

 

1) Sustainability and ESG Reporting Software 

 

Video 2: SAP Sustainability Control Tower – Holistic Steering and ESG Reporting

 

Figure 2: The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution

 

SAP allows companies and organizations to connect their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) data and steer their organizations towards more sustainable and efficient operations, thereby also helping to achieve national and international climate goals. The SAP ESG software was designed in a holistic way and provides streamlined reporting as well as valuable insights for decision-makers and stakeholders. Furthermore, it allows for the full integration of the organization’s stakeholders, like suppliers, into the system. This also means that governments theoretically can implement the solution nation-wide or across different governmental areas for managing everything around sustainability with one central software platform. Apart from that, the software generally allows D&S organizations to measure, communicate, and achieve corporate sustainability goals to the broader public. Moreover, the sustainability software is integrable across various different systems, even across the respective organization or company. This allows bringing sustainability, safety, risk, compliance, asset, and people data together in one place for enabling comprehensive sustainability reporting and analysis.

 

2) SAP Climate Action Solutions

 

Video 3: Transactional Carbon Accounting with SAP

 

Figure 3: Measuring emissions with the SAP Sustainability Management solutions

 

The SAP Climate Action Solutions allow companies and organizations to decarbonize their entire value chain from end-to-end to become climate-neutral organizations. This is made possible by integrating actual business transactions with carbon accounting software, emissions and compliance management, carbon footprint calculations, e-mobility, and transportation logistics. The solution includes services like:

  • Real-time decision making with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
  • SAP Environment, Health and Safety Management
  • and many more services

 

3) Circular Economy Software 

 

Video 4: Circular Design with SAP

 

Figure 4: The SAP Circular Economy solutions

 

The SAP Circular Economy solutions allow companies and organizations to integrate the concept of circularity into their organization. Circularity thereby allows them to reduce the use of resources and materials, such as plastic, as much as possible. In addition, the concept aims to eliminate waste and circulate materials. Our Circular Economy solutions thereby consider all these requirements and allow managing circularity holistically. This includes circularity in supply chains, production, and all other applicable organizational processes. Especially worth mentioning in this regard is the possibility to use blockchain technology for capturing the whole material provenance from raw materials to finished products. Other promising features of the circular economy solutions are, for instance, the ability to create circular logistic flows for transport containers, pallets, and other forms of packaging.

 

4) Social Responsibility Solutions

 

Video 5: Social Responsibility for Zero Inequality with SAP

 

Figure 5: The EcoVadis API Connector for SAP Ariba

 

The SAP Social Responsibility solutions allow companies and organizations to manage their impact on people and society centrally with one software package. This involves the ability to consider social and environmental requirements across the entire value and supply chains of your organization. Moreover, it encompasses the ability to proactively ensure health, safety, as well as labor rights within the entire organization, but also to establish workforce sustainability by creating equal treatment and opportunities like, for instance, guaranteeing equal pay for men and women. In addition, SAP within this thematic area offers D&S organizations a human rights due diligence software that allows them to enforce human rights across all supply chains.

 

Artificial Intelligence Functions and Features

 

SAP is planning to integrate Artificial Intelligence into all eligible software solutions. For instance, it has set a goal to embed its Generative AI-powered Joule Copilot into 80% of the most used tasks by the end of 2024. However, various super-helpful AI solutions are already available, including those for SAP Sustainability Management solutions, where our customers can benefit from:

 

- AI for Emission Factor Management

(Demo Video: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Features)

Our customers can simplify emission factor management by using the SAP Business AI-powered automation function, which is directly integrated into the solution. This improves result accuracy and reduces manual labor.

 

- AI for ESG Report Generation

The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution allows users to record, report, and act on their diverse sustainability data. SAP has recently enhanced this solution by integrating an AI feature for the automated creation of ESG reports. This streamlines the reporting process, saving valuable time and money.

 

 

The Security Architecture of the SAP Sustainability Management Solutions

 

The SAP Sustainability Management software suite is built on an end-to-end security architecture that meets the demands of even the most security-conscious companies and organizations around the world. This includes:

 

- Cloud-Based Security

The sustainability software solutions are built with a security-first cloud approach. The cloud solution is based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), which includes strict functionalities for access control and data encryption. Additionally, SAP continuously develops its products and distributes regular software updates and upgrades to its clients.

 

- Data Integration and Auditing

SAP Sustainability Management is directly integrated or integrable into core Enterprise Resource Planning processes, making all data directly traceable and auditable.

 

- AI-Enabled Automation

Artificial Intelligence technology plays an important role through the SAP Joule Copilot and various embedded AI functions within SAP applications. It is also crucial for the security of the SAP Sustainability Management solutions. AI is used for process automation, eliminating the risk of human error, and for continuous monitoring of data and processes. If the system detects any security threat, it will directly inform the system user, who can then investigate and resolve the issue.

 

- Flexible and Extensible Architecture

SAP Sustainability Control Tower, one of the core sustainability solutions, offers clients maximum flexibility by allowing them to integrate custom content and create customized solutions.

 

- Integration of Regulatory Standards for Compliance

The SAP Sustainability Management solutions allow the incorporation of all major regulatory frameworks and legal requirements into the system. Examples for this include for instance the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Defense and Security organizations can also integrate their own sustainability guidelines, such as national and international standards or guidelines from defense organizations like NATO.

 

 

Conclusion

 

In conclusion, this blog entry has shown that sustainability is crucial for Defense and Security (D&S) organizations, not only for achieving organizational, national, or international climate goals but also for creating significant opportunities for organizational realignment towards a greener orientation. By adopting sustainable practices, D&S organizations can contribute to combating global warming while simultaneously saving significant costs on fuel consumption, heating systems, and weapon systems, without sacrificing their core defense capabilities. It is important to note the significant positive influence such transformations can have on public perception and the innovative strength of individual military organizations and the entire D&S industry. A good example is the automotive industry, which, like many other industries, has undergone significant transformation in recent years with the shift to electric drives, now offering more modern and technologically advanced products, that are also more environmentally friendly. António Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations, aptly stated, “The climate emergency is a race we are losing, but it is a race we can win.” This call to action resonates deeply within the defense sector, urging all stakeholders to embrace sustainable practices and technologies. However, setting climate goals and creating corresponding guidelines is one thing, but achieving them another. That is why D&S organizations need to develop holistic sustainability concepts in partnership with key stakeholders, such as governments, suppliers, defense organizations, or supranational organizations like the European Union. They also need to measure, manage, and report their progress. This is where SAP’s cutting-edge SAP Sustainability Management solutions come into play. The software package covers all typical sustainability requirements for D&S organizations and includes various supplementary tools and features, such as ESG reporting, circular economy solutions, social responsibility, health and safety solutions, and innovative AI solutions. In conclusion, by establishing climate and sustainability action plans and utilizing powerful sustainability management solutions like SAP Sustainability Management, D&S organizations can set a powerful example, proving that security and sustainability can go hand in hand. Let’s embark on this journey together towards a greener, more sustainable future for everyone.

 

 

Sources

 

Berlin Energy and Climate Protection Programme 2030. (2018, January, 25). Berlin.de.

Climate change considerations for EU. (2022, June). European Parliament.

Climate-Neutral Berlin 2045. (2021, August). Berlin.de.

Decarbonize the military — mandate emissions reporting. (2022, November, 02). Nature.

Decarbonizing defense carbon emissions . (2021, July, 01). McKinsey.

Department of Defense Plan to Reduce Greenhouse Gas Emissions. (2023, June 16). US Department of Defense.

Embracing sustainability using the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework. (2024, May, 14). SAP Community.

Explore the SAP Sustainability Solutions. (2024, May, 14). SAP Community.

London Fire Brigade Sustainable Development Strategy 2023-2025 . (2023, May, 16). London Fire Brigade.

Master Guide: SAP Sustainability Performance. (2016, October, 24). SAP Help.

NATO Climate Change and Security Action Plan. (2021, June, 14). NATO.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower Update: Holistic ESG . (2022, November). SAP News.

SAP Sustainability Solutions. (2024, October). SAP.

Schwarzenegger takes center stage on warming. (2006, September, 27). NBC News.

Strategic Decision Paths for Managing Corporate Carbon Footprint . (2024, October, 02.). SAP Community.

The Growing Climate Stakes for the Defense Industry. (2021, September, 10). Boston Consulting Group.

Treibhausgas-Emissionen der Bundeswehr gestiegen. (2022, May, 19). Bundeswehr Journal.

U.S. Department of Defense Plan to Reduce Greenhouse Gas Emissions. (2023, June 16). U.S. Department of Defense.

Your London Fire Brigade 2023-2029. (2023, January). London Fire Brigade.

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Automation Pilot Q3 2024
entry.content:
In this blog post, we’ll dive into the latest updates and features in SAP Automation Pilot, designed to further enhance your automation capabilities and streamline operational workflows. Discover how these improvements can help you optimize and streamline your tasks for greater efficiency.

Content Generation Assistant - GlobalAvailability

We’re happy to announce that our Generative AI (GenAI) capability in SAP Automation Pilot reached General Availability globally on September 20th! With the Content Generation Assistant, users can now leverage AI to generate commands and workflows using natural language prompts, simplifying the automation process and minimizing manual effort.

 

For more information on this exciting update, you can check out the full blog post here.

Scheduled Executions Generation

With the latest updates, the Content Generation Assistant now supports generation of scheduled executions in SAP Automation Pilot with just a single prompt. The assistant automatically creates executions set to run at specific times, simplifying workflows and reducing manual tasks. This feature enhances flexibility and control, making operations more streamlined and easier to manage.

Remote Work Processor - Standalone Mode

The Remote Work Processor in SAP Automation Pilot can now operate in standalone mode. This lightweight agent allows users to securely make network requests in restricted environments, like private networks or on-premise systems with limited internet access. By delegating network requests to the agent, Automation Pilot can access internal resources, enabling automation in isolated or fenced-off systems. This enhancement significantly improves the tool's flexibility, making it easier to manage and automate tasks in hybrid and complex network setups.

UI Enhancements: Improved Dialogs and Expression Suggestions

The revamped Input and Output dialogs in our UI now feature a more organized structure, making it much easier to handle large JSON arrays or objects. Enhanced formatting options improve the readability of complex data, and users can hide specific fields for a more cleaner view.

 In addition to the updated Input and Output dialogs, we’ve also enhanced the Key Management Dialogs with a simplified, dynamic view. Depending on the type of data you’re working with, the dialog expands to display relevant fields, making it more flexible and user-friendly.

 

We’re also excited to announce that the Expression suggestions are now supported across all smart view text areas, further streamlining the user experience.

Content Connectors

With Content Connectors in SAP Automation Pilot, users can now back up their content directly to GitHub, either manually or on a scheduled six-hour basis. You can configure backups by selecting specific catalogues and applying tags to filter what gets backed up. Additionally, backups can be saved as individual files for each command and input or consolidated into a single file. The intuitive interface simplifies the process, making it easy to organize and safeguard your automation content efficiently.

New SAP Alert Notification service Events for Commands and Inputs

SAP Automation Pilot can now produce events for key actions on commands and inputs including:

  • Command Created, Updated, Deleted: Notifications for any creation, modification, or deletion of commands.
  • Input Created, Updated, Deleted: Real-time alerts when inputs are added, changed, or removed.

These new event notifications provide instant visibility into important updates, helping teams maintain greater control and transparency in shared environments. Whether it’s for improved collaboration or operational oversight, these alerts make monitoring changes more seamless and efficient.

Check out how to configure SAP Automation Pilot to produce these events here. 

New command in AI Core Catalog: GenerateGpt4OmniCompletion

Introducing a new command in the AI Core Catalog: GenerateGpt4OmniCompletion in SAP Automation Pilot. This command allows you to generate completions using GPT-4o for the SAP AI Core's generative AI hub.

Additionally, both the GenerateGpt4OmniCompletion and GenerateGpt35TurboCompletion commands now support response formatting, offering a cleaner and more structured view of the data, enhancing readability and usability.

New Example Scenario: Advanced Availability Check

The Advanced Availability Check monitors service availability and reports results to Dynatrace for SLA tracking and data visualization. In this example, the HTTP Bin service is used for testing, but it can be adapted for various services based on customer needs. Availability checks can also be scheduled at regular intervals using Automation Pilot’s Scheduled Executions feature. This provides a robust mechanism for continuous service monitoring, improving operational efficiency and reliability.

Check out the example in our Github repo with samples and try it. 

How to stay tuned to recent and upcoming innovations? 

  • What’s New in SAP Automation Pilot
  • SAP Road Map Explorer for SAP Automation Pilot.
  • Release Navigator for SAP Automation Pilot easing you the way to find product release related notes, blogposts, and webpages.
  • Public GitHub Automation Pilot sample use cases;

entry.title: Proxy cannot be reached while adding subaccount in Cloud Connector
entry.content:
Hi Folks,

Recently, I had a issue in the cloud connector version 2.17.1

I have installed the Cloud connector in my personal laptop and trying to add the BTP sub account. It was giving error "Proxy Cannot be reached".

 

After analyzing the issue, I got to know that in this version it is trying to check the proxy in the system and it is failing.

 

Solution:  Uninstall the Version 2.17.1 and install the Version 2.16.2 for window and other operating systems.

The version is available on the SAP Site: https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/#cloud

After installing this version, issue will solve.

 

Thanks.

Regards,

Rohit Gera

entry.title: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System
entry.content:
Introduction

From 2402 Release, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition added a new system monitoring capability, collectively called the Technical Monitoring Cockpit for monitoring the ABAP system.  The Cockpit provides a seamless connection between your system workload and single ABAP statistics records (main records and sub-records) down to SQL statements and their prepared plan.  Now you have a good starting point to conduct performance analysis on the SAP S/4HANA system in case of high response times, especially when you are an administrator or a developer. 

As this Technical Monitoring Cockpit is not unique to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and the Business Technology Platform, the discussion here applies to them as well.

I plan to tackle this topic in a series of blogs in bite sizes so that you can easily consume them during your day-to-day work.

  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through: this blog provides a guided tour of the Cockpit to readers, walkthrough (clicking through) various functions and resolve some confusions.
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts: this blog explains what metrics we are looking for in the Cockpit, and how they can be helpful to you, especially when something seems suspicious. 
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System Workload:  this blog discusses the approach as an administrator/developer to analyze the system performance daily.
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Analyzing Performance with A Customized Profile (to be published):  this blog discusses how to take the advantage of setting up a profile to record system data you are interested, hence analyze the data when issues surface.

In my previous two blogs of this Technical Monitoring Cockpit series, I explained how to click through these apps and the key concepts, now let’s put them into use in your own system: how to do a system analysis as an administrator/developer daily.

 

System Workload

When talking about the system monitoring, the first entry is the System Workload app (see my previous blog on how to access it).

The below screen System Workload: Response Time Contribution gives a glimpse on how the system is utilized in the past 24 hours (Note: my most business users are in Asia, so the heavy workload appears in my midnight to early morning since I am located in US East Coast.)

  • During the past 24 hours (12 pm on Oct. 9 to 12 pm on Oct. 10), heavy workload happened around 2 am to 6 am.
  • The peak Response Time Contribution reached 360 seconds.
  • If I am curious on the peak Response Time Contribution, I can move the Time Slider to left. Comparing with two graphs of Oct. 8 vs. 9, we found
    • The peak Response Time Contribution is about the same, 300+ seconds, although they occurred at a different time slot.
    • When focusing on the highest Response Time Contribution, the pattern is similar with three high components: the Roll Wait Time, the ABAP Processing Time and the HANA Execution Time.

The Peak Response Time Contribution from 12 pm to 12 pm on Oct. 9

 

The Peak Response Time Contribution from 12 pm to 12 pm on Oct. 8

 

Now let’s narrow the time range to 2:30 – 3:15 am. I want to find out the reason of high Roll Wait Time, ABAP Processing Time and HANA Execution Time.  By clicking on Roll Wait Time in the Legend, we can see the Remote Function Call (RFC) is the main contributor, almost 93% ((56.36+56.24+49.39+8.99+8.97+8.27)/201.76). The top three programs are QDEST_RUN, QDEST_RUN_DESTINATION and ARFC_RUN_NOWAIT.

Explore the Cause of Long Roll Wait Time

 

The Roll Wait Time represents a time for which the roll context was rolled out. In the below figure, it is a major contributor to an RFC Process or waiting time until the results are returned from an external system.  This matches with the nature of an RFC call, i.e., you have no control over an external system on how fast it returns the result to you.

Contributors to the Server Response Time in a Synchronous RFC Processing (from SAP Help)

 

Now I click on the ABAP Processing Time in the Legend, the top ten contributors are listed in the table.  The top one is to run a program called /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources in an OData V2 type request.

Explore the Cause of Long ABAP Processing Time

 

Go to the details (right most > button of the record), we can see this program was run seven times as listed in the table.  The processing time is quite even, between 1.46 to 2.04 seconds.

ABAP Processing Time of Running Program /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources (At 10-Minute Intervals)

 

In the blog Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Key Concepts, I explained how the ABAP Statistics Data are collected: one minute at a time on those completed work processes (look back), and data are aggregated. How about we look at the data in a different granularity?  For example, select As Collected? That is a different view (see below).  However, we still cannot pin down which vertical purple bar belongs to which of these seven runs.

ABAP Processing Time of Running Program /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources (As Collected)

 

To get the details of each run, I click on the Details button at the right of the top row. This brings me to the ABAP Statistics Record screen with more information about this record.

In the tab Server Response Time Contribution, it provides all the metric data of this program. The large portion of the time is spent on the ABAP Processing and HANA Execution.

ABAP Statistics Record – Server Response Time Contribution

 

When I see the Database Network Time is 148.54 milliseconds, it tells me there are many trips to the database.  I want to explore further by clicking on the tab Data Connections. There are 26,326 database calls.  This confirms a lot of trips were made to the database.

ABAP Statistics Record – Database Connections

 

By clicking on the Details button again, we can see the Database Connection Details screen.  Although there are 26,326 logical database requests, only 333 physical database requests happened. From database point of view, it is efficient as most data are cached.

Database Connection Details

 

You can also explore the SQL statements associated with this long ABAP Processing Time by clicking on the tab SQL Statements.  When clicking on the first row of the program, you can see the details of this SQL statement.

SQL Statement Details

 

Sampled Work Process Data

In the blog Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Key Concepts, I discussed there are two perspectives of exploring system performance.  One is the System Workload, which has data at every minute on those completed work processes.  Another one is called Sampled Work Process Data.  It is looking at data at every second including those currently running work processes. I use this perspective to identify the running programs after I notice a peak resource consumption from System Workload.  Let me explain that.

The below figure displays the Sampled Work Process Data at the same time range as above, from 12 pm on Oct. 8 to 12 pm on Oct. 9.

Sampled Work Process Data (from 12 pm on Oct. 8 to 12 pm on Oct. 9)

 

It is tricky to isolate these colorful bars around 3 am.  If you try to narrow down to 2:30 – 3:15 am, you don’t see any data.  So, you have to enlarge the time range, for example 18:15 – 8:37. After applying filter of OData V2, I got the below chart and table.

Sampled Work Process Data: ABAP CPU Time (18:15 – 8:37)

 

In the previous section, I was investigating a high ABAP CPU Time with the following information:

  • Request Entry Type: OData V2
  • Request Entry Name: /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001
  • Program Context: /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources
  • Start Time: 2024-10-09 02:34:57.92458200

 

Now I click on the first row with the same -  Request Entry Name: /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001.  A new screen opens with a time series of this Program Context but different Program Name. When I focus on the time range of 2:34:57.92458200, there are three programs running in that vicinity, namely:

  • /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP
  • CL_ACMRT_KERNEL_PROXY=========CP
  • /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP

Request Entry Point: OData V2 and /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001

 

Take the last program /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP as an example. I click on the Details button on the right. It reveals the Single Work Process Sample screen. I can review the Response Time and other information.

Single Work Process Sample – Work Process Metrics

 

When clicking the “ABAP Statistics Record” button, the ABAP Statistics for this Request Entry Point becomes available. Please keep in mind that this Record is an aggregated information of this Request Entry Point in the past minute, no longer at that second. But it is still good enough for you to isolate the program you are interested in.

ABAP Statistics Record for Program /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP

 

You can do the same for other two programs: /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP and CL_ACMRT_KERNEL_PROXY=========CP.

 

Conclusion

This blog discusses a daily routine work for an administrator or a developer on how to discover a high resource consumption activity, and find out the program causes that by using both the System Workload and Sampled Work Process apps.

 

References

  • SAP Help: Monitoring the System Workload
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts 
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System Workload
  • Blog: Analyzing performance degradations in the ABAP environment in the Cloud

entry.title: Guided Experiences powered by SAP Build
entry.content:
Table of Content

  • Motivation for Guided Experiences

  • New Guided Process Modelling: Advanced Automation and Flexibility

  • Connecting Guided Processes as Wizard Templates in SAP Build Work Zone

  • Conclusion – SAP Build Guided Experiences

  • What's Next?

  • Additional Read

Motivation for Guided Experiences

Guided experiences are designed to simplify multi-step business processes by breaking them down into manageable and intuitive steps. Each step can be enriched with various types of content, including videos, documents, forms, tasks, UI cards, and apps to provide users with all the necessary information to complete each step. Steps can be classified as optional or mandatory, ensuring critical tasks are completed while allowing flexibility for other actions. Users don't need to worry about finishing all the steps at once as the progress is saved, enabling them to resume from where they left off. Guided experiences can be used for various complex processes such as parental leave, benefits enrolment, employee relocation, new hires onboarding or suppliers onboarding, training and enablement, and browsing and purchasing electronic equipment. To learn more about the background & motivation around guided experiences, please see our previous blog about this capability.

New Guided Process Modelling: Advanced Automation and Flexibility

The major enhancement in the October 2024 release is the ability to model guided processes directly in SAP Build Process Automation, bringing powerful process automation to your SAP Build projects. The following key aspects have been added:

Guided Process Artefact

The new ‘Guided Process’ artefact allows you to design and manage structured, multi-step processes within SAP Build Process Automation. This feature is perfect for creating more dynamic and reusable workflows.

Stages and Steps 

Following the structure introduced for the wizard layout, ‘Guided Processes’ equally allow to create stages & steps within each stage.  Additionally, these process steps can be designed to be sequential or parallel flows, allowing for both straightforward and complex workflows.

Create a StageAdd a Step to a Stage

 

Note: with this release, only SAP Build Work Zone is supported as ‘Step UI’; meaning the step itself needs to designed via the Work Zone wizard layout editor (for example to add UI integration cards).

Advanced Logic Integration

To create more sophisticated processes as basis for your guided experience, branches can be used to structure the overall guided process and duration checks can be incorporated via wait for duration to handle conditional starting of stages. Furthermore, subprocesses can be used to handle more complex requirements, such as retrieving hire date information.

Easy Deployment to SAP Build Work Zone

Once the guided process is modelled, it can be deployed to SAP Build Work Zone as a wizard layout template, ensuring easy availability for end-users. This provides a seamless transition from process design to execution.

Note: the deployment to SAP Build Work Zone is performed on behalf of the logged-in user via the automatically created destination pointing to DWS as per the following guide.

Shared Environments in SAP Build

This new ‘Guided Process’ artefact is only supported in shared environments, not in the default / public one. To learn more about shared environments in SAP Build, please refer to the following blog post.

Connecting Guided Processes as Wizard Templates in SAP Build Work Zone

In addition to modelling ‘wizard layouts’ directly in SAP Build Work Zone (which is now available via the “New Wizard” option)...

Add Wizard LayoutSelect Process for WizardOption for Standalone Wizard

 

… it is now possible to select ‘Guided Processes’ modelled in SAP Build Process Automation as the underlying structure when creating guided experiences.

Guided Process-based Wizards

Users can now select one of previously deployed guided processes they have access to. These serve as the template the wizard layout within SAP Build Work Zone.

Note: While modelling & deploying guided processes via the SAP Build Lobby requires access to the ‘advanced user’ license in SAP Build Process Automation, that is not the case for consuming deployed processes. For doing so, the user requires the following permissions:

  • Workpage edit permissions in Work Zone to edit the workpage, add wizard layouts,
  • ProcessAutomationParticipant role collection
  • 'Execute' permission for shared environment (Share an Environment | SAP Help Portal)

Note: creating wizard layouts based on ‘guided processes’ is currently released in preview environments of SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition / SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone and will become available in the non-preview environments with the subsequent release.

Auto-generated Wizard Structure

When selecting a guided process, the wizard layout is automatically generated based on the process definition, saving time and ensuring consistency.

Design Wizard with Widgets & UI Cards

Just like with a manually-created one, you can customize guided process-based wizard layouts using different widgets and UI cards, providing flexibility in designing the user experience.

  • Adding Widgets / Cards to each step: 
  • Example Step with Image widget:

Note: Already in a previous release, UI cards have been enhanced to interact with wizard layouts in SAP Build Work Zone, for instance to enforce users taking certain actions. Details and code examples for such UI cards are available via our central SAP Build Work Zone GitHub sample repository.

Process Instance Creation

When a user starts a wizard based on a guided process, an instance of that process is started automatically, allowing execution & resuming of the steps according to the defined process structure.

Conclusion – SAP Build Guided Experiences

This streamlined integration reduces development time and ensures that teams can leverage the power of SAP Build Process Automation alongside SAP Build Work Zone bringing exciting possibilities for creating powerful & content-rich guided experiences. These innovations not only simplify process design and execution but also ensure that you can deploy efficient, scalable guided experiences tailored to your unique needs – bringing different capabilities from SAP Build together via a deeply integrated, end-to-end experience!

What’s Next?

Looking ahead, we are planning to introduce additional features to further enhance guided experiences. These upcoming capabilities aim to add additional flexibility for creating, designing & operating guided experiences with SAP Build.  Here's a sneak peek at what's planned (*):

Spoiler
  • Repeating Guided Experiences
  • Starting Multiple Instances of the same Guided Experience
  • Support for more Step UIs, including Forms
  • Integration with Process Visibility for Monitoring
  • […]

In addition to those additional enhancements, we are also working on providing additional pre-built business content (packages) for guided experiences (*), such as onboarding in context of SAP SuccessFactors. Stay tuned as these new features roll out, offering even more ways to optimize your guided experiences and streamline your business processes!

(*) Disclaimer: Please note that the roadmap items shared here are subject to change and may not be delivered as initially planned. The information provided is for informational purposes only and does not constitute a commitment by SAP. As always, the best resource to learn about upcoming capabilities is the central roadmap explorer.

Additional Read

To learn more about the topic covered in this blog post, please also refer to the following assets & follow us on SAP community.

  • Related Blog Posts:
    • What’s new with SAP Build Work Zone guided experiences
    • SAP Build Process Automation - Introducing Environments
  • Learning Journeys & Certification: 
    • Work Zone: Implement and Administer SAP Build Work Zone | SAP Learning
    • Process Automation: Creating Processes and Automations with SAP Build Process Automation
  • Reference Architectures:
    • https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/refArchDetail/ref-arch-sap-build-work-zone
    • https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/refArchDetail/ref-arch-build-process-automation
  • SAP Help guides for more details:
    • Work Zone Preview: https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-advanced-edition/sap-build-work-zone-advanced-edition/what... (will move to regular admin guide with next release)
    • Process Automation: Create a Guided Process
  • GitHub Repository for Work Zone Examples: https://github.com/SAP-samples/build-workzone-integration/tree/main/advanced/wizard

entry.title: Try it out now : SAP Dynamic Forms generally available now in SAP BTP Trail Account
entry.content:
